sections.title: Clean and Bright
sections.content_summary: This section introduces daily hygiene habits — brushing teeth, showering, combing hair, washing hands with soap, trimming nails — through a self-check questionnaire. Students write down their full daily routine from waking to sleeping and audit whether they brush twice a day, rinse after meals, and wash hands after toilet use and outdoor play. The section frames these as habits that keep both body and mind healthy.

tasks.task_text: Write down the activities that you do every day, from the time you wake up, till you go to sleep. Your list should include: Take a shower, Wash your hands with soap, Brush your teeth, Drink 6–8 glasses of water, Eat food, Sleep well for at least 8 hours total, Play outside.

sections.title: How do we Brush our Teeth?
sections.content_summary: Through the story of Moyna and her Dadu, the section introduces the Indian tradition of datun — using neem, karanj, or babool twigs to clean teeth — as the origin of the modern toothbrush concept. Students are encouraged to try a datun and interview elders about what they used to wash hair, body and clothes in the past. An eco-friendly home-cleaner recipe using orange/lemon peels, cloves, and white vinegar introduces traditional household cleaning as a two-week making project, reflecting IKS knowledge of natural cleaning substances like reetha and shikakai.

tasks.task_text: Making a home-cleaner using orange and lemon peels: Put peels of 8–12 oranges or lemons, ten whole cloves (laung) or 2–3 bay leaves (tejpatta) (optional), and two cups of white vinegar in a half-litre transparent container. Close the lid, keep in a sunny area for about two weeks, occasionally shaking the mixture. Strain the liquid after two weeks. Mix a cup of the liquid in one bucket of water to clean floors and bathrooms.
Ask your grandparents or any elderly person in your neighbourhood — What did they use to wash their hair, body and clothes and clean their teeth when they were young? Can these things be found now?

sections.title: Playing Outdoors and Indoors too
sections.content_summary: The section explains the physical effects of exercise — faster breathing, redness in cheeks, sweating — as signs that the heart is working harder, making the body stronger. Through Activity 2 (Hop, skip, jump!), children mimic animal movements and use a skipping rope, learning that play is a form of exercise. An example of Harpreet's grandmother who plays chess despite knee pain illustrates that both body and mind need activity. Students identify traditional Indian games (spinning top, ludo, snakes-and-ladders, carrom, and games in a word-search) and are encouraged to teach each other.

tasks.task_text: Hop, skip, jump! Try the following moves: Walk like a duck. Jump like a frog. Run like a cat. Take a skipping rope and count as you jump. Do this with your friends and see who can jump the maximum number of times. Then invite a friend to start jumping after you skip 10 times or after you say a short poem as you skip.

sections.title: Playing Safely
sections.content_summary: This section develops safety awareness during play by asking students to judge five environments — a busy road, a park, a schoolground, a roof without railings, and a construction site — as safe or unsafe, and to explain their reasoning. Stranger-danger scenarios are presented through comic dialogues where children refuse sweets, a stranger's offer to be picked up, and a lift in a car — building civic self-protection habits. Students are reminded to pay attention to their surroundings at all times.

tasks.task_text: Can you identify the games shown here? Have you played them before?
Can you find the names of all the games hidden here? Are any of these your favourites? One has been done for you.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The reflection section consolidates the chapter's health themes through three tasks. The Write task (weekly health table) asks students to track seven daily indicators — teeth brushing, toilet use, shower, sleep, breakfast, screen time, outdoor play, and how they feel — for a full week, building self-monitoring habits. The Draw task (24-slice circle) represents a full day's 24 hours coloured by activity, connecting health time-management to proportional thinking. The Discuss task asks each child to commit to one stamina-building activity and do it with friends twice a week.

tasks.task_text: Make your weekly health table. Track your activities for a week and observe how you feel: Did I brush my teeth twice? Did I use the toilet in the morning? Did I take shower? How much time did I sleep? What did I have for breakfast? How long did I watch TV or play on the phone? How long did I play outdoors? How did it make me feel?

Draw a big circle in your notebook and divide it into 24 slices. Imagine that each slice is one hour of the day. Now colour these slices differently based on the number of hours you spend doing different activities in one entire day of 24 hours.

Think of one activity you would like to do at least twice a week to improve your stamina. It could be running, jumping, climbing stairs or anything else. Discuss it with your friends and try doing such activities together.

dual_strand.natural: Hygiene, exercise and physical health — daily habits that keep the body clean, strong and disease-free
dual_strand.human_cultural: Traditional Indian cleaning practices (datun, reetha, shikakai), traditional games, eco-friendly home-cleaner making
